attack_types.name: Reentrancy
attack_types.description: Re-enter a function before state updates, draining funds recursively

attack_types.name: Access Control Missing
attack_types.description: Privileged functions lack onlyOwner or role checks, anyone can call

attack_types.name: Integer Overflow / Underflow
attack_types.description: Wrap uint256 boundaries to produce unintended large or zero values

attack_types.name: Unchecked Return Value
attack_types.description: transfer() or call() return value ignored, failure silently continues

attack_types.name: Delegatecall Injection
attack_types.description: Proxy delegates to attacker-controlled implementation, storage overwritten

attack_types.name: Read-Only Reentrancy
attack_types.description: View function called mid-execution while state is inconsistent across protocols

attack_types.name: Tx.Origin Phishing
attack_types.description: Contract uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender, attacker tricks user via malicious contract

attack_types.name: Self-Destruct Exploit
attack_types.description: Force-send ETH via selfdestruct breaking balance-based invariants

attack_types.name: Flash Loan Attack
attack_types.description: Borrow uncollateralized funds within single tx to manipulate state or drain liquidity

attack_types.name: Price Oracle Manipulation
attack_types.description: Skew AMM spot price used as oracle to borrow against inflated collateral

attack_types.name: Donation / Inflation Attack
attack_types.description: Donate tokens directly to vault to inflate share price, grief depositors

attack_types.name: Slippage / Deadline Missing
attack_types.description: Swap with no slippage protection, trivially sandwichable

attack_types.name: Governance Attack
attack_types.description: Acquire voting power via flash loan to pass malicious proposals

attack_types.name: Signature Replay
attack_types.description: Reuse valid signature on different chain or context without chainId/nonce check

attack_types.name: Sandwich Attack
attack_types.description: Frontrun and backrun victim tx to profit from their price impact

attack_types.name: Frontrunning
attack_types.description: Copy pending tx with higher gas to execute first and steal opportunity

attack_types.name: Cross-Chain Bridge Exploit
attack_types.description: Forge or replay messages between chains to mint unbacked tokens

attack_types.name: Private Key Compromise
attack_types.description: Leaked deployer key used to upgrade contract or drain treasury

attack_types.name: Supply Chain
attack_types.description: Malicious package mimics legitimate one, injects key exfiltration at build time

attack_types.name: Role-Play Attack
attack_types.description: Attacker plays multiple roles (e.g., LP and trader) within a single protocol to exploit internal accounting logic

attack_types.name: Oracle Misconfiguration
attack_types.description: Safety-layer oracle parameters drift out of sync with market reality, causing incorrect pricing and unwarranted liquidations

attack_types.name: ZK Proof Misconfiguration
attack_types.web2_equivalent: Incomplete PKI ceremony / default credentials shipped to production
attack_types.description: Incomplete or skipped trusted setup ceremony leaves ZK verifier accepting any proof, enabling forged withdrawals

exploits.description: Compromised signing infrastructure via supply chain attack on Safe multisig UI
exploits.detailed_narrative: On February 21, 2025, Bybit lost approximately 401,347 ETH (~$1.4B) from its multisig cold wallet in the largest single-theft crypto hack to date, attributed to North Korea's Lazarus Group. (1) The attackers compromised a Safe{Wallet} developer's machine via social engineering and malware, gaining access to the Safe UI codebase hosted at app.safe.global. (2) They injected malicious JavaScript specifically targeting Bybit's cold wallet signing flow — the payload was inert for all other Safe users and only activated when Bybit's signers loaded the interface. (3) When Bybit signers initiated a routine ~30,000 ETH transfer to a hot wallet, the tampered UI silently replaced the transaction payload with a disguised delegatecall. (4) The delegatecall overwrote the cold wallet proxy's implementation slot (storage slot 0) with an attacker-controlled contract at 0xbDd077f651EBe7f7b3cE16fe5F2b025BE2969516, redirecting all future logic to the attacker. (5) With the proxy now under their control, the attackers drained the entire 401,347 ETH balance in a single atomic execution. (6) Funds were rapidly laundered across DEXes, mixers, and cross-chain bridges. The attack succeeded because the UI tampering bypassed the multisig visual verification process — signers approved what appeared legitimate but never saw the real on-chain payload.

exploits.description: Missing access control on bridge relay function allowed unauthorized cross-chain message execution
exploits.detailed_narrative: On February 8, 2026, CrossCurve's bridge infrastructure was exploited for approximately $3M due to missing access-control checks on the Axelar relay path. (1) The ReceiverAxelar contract's expressExecute-like functions were intended to process only messages originating from the Axelar network, but they lacked robust origin verification and signature checks. (2) The attacker crafted malicious cross-chain messages that mimicked legitimate Axelar payloads, bypassing the weak validation logic. (3) These forged messages instructed the ReceiverAxelar contract to release specified token amounts to attacker-controlled addresses. (4) The ReceiverAxelar then called the PortalV2 contract to unlock liquidity, believing a valid deposit had occurred on the source chain — no corresponding deposit ever existed. (5) The attacker repeated this across every supported chain, systematically draining liquidity pools on each. (6) After extraction, the stolen tokens were swapped into liquid assets and laundered across multiple protocols. The team responded by shutting down bridge components. No smart-contract bug bounty or formal verification had caught the missing access-control checks on the relay path.

exploits.description: Liquidity math error from unchecked arithmetic caused inflated token minting
exploits.detailed_narrative: Truebit's Purchase contract (deployed 2021, unverified bytecode, Solidity 0.5.3) contained an unprotected addition in getPurchasePrice(uint256 amount). SafeMath guarded mul/div but not this addition, allowing overflow on absurdly large inputs. Step-by-step: (1) Attacker called getPurchasePrice() with an astronomical TRU amount → overflow returned near-zero ETH cost. (2) Minted 240+ million TRU for pennies via the mint function. (3) Approved/transferred tokens to the Purchase contract. (4) Burned them at the 12.5% buyback rate, receiving real ETH. (5) Repeated the mint-burn cycle 5 times atomically in one tx (no price impact between steps). Total: 8,535 ETH drained into Tornado Cash. The same attacker wallet had previously hit Sparkle (5 ETH). Funding traced to Rhino.fi (Nov/Dec 2025). A copycat exploited the now-public vuln for ~$250K. TRU token went to zero. The contract had no supply cap, no per-tx limits, and had sat dormant as a 'rug zone' since 2021. Attacker contract: 0x1de399967b206e446b4e9aeeb3cb0a0991bf11b8; original wallet: 0x6c8ec8f14be7c01672d31cfa5f2cefeab2562b50.

exploits.description: Oracle price manipulation allowed borrowing against inflated collateral values
exploits.detailed_narrative: YieldBlox DAO lending pool (on Blend V2, Stellar) used the Reflector VWAP oracle for USTRY/USDC collateral pricing. The market had near-zero liquidity after the only market maker withdrew. Attacker: (1) Placed a sell offer for USTRY at 501 USDC per token (100x real price ~$1.06). (2) Used a second account to buy 0.05 USTRY against that offer in one trade, dominating the oracle's 15-minute window. (3) Reflector oracle reported ~$106.74 price. (4) Protocol accepted the inflated valuation and allowed massive borrowing: 1,000,196 USDC + 61,249,278 XLM (~$10.97M total). Funds withdrawn in two blocks. $7.2M later frozen by Stellar Tier-1 validators; ~$3M unrecovered. No circuit breakers, no liquidity-depth requirements, no sanity checks on oracle inputs. Classic thin-market manipulation exploiting a configuration that routed pricing through an illiquid DEX pair.

exploits.description: Bridge message replay allowed minting unbacked wrapped tokens on destination chain
exploits.detailed_narrative: The attacker first compromised the private key of the Ethereum-side Validator contract owner. Using that key: (1) Executed a malicious upgrade to the Validator contract, stripping validation and signature checks. (2) Gained admin control over TokenSafe (reserves) and MintPool contracts. (3) Drained ~$4.3M in real tokens from TokenSafe using admin privileges. (4) Minted ~111 million unbacked CIOTX tokens via MintPool. Most minted tokens were frozen on IoTeX/Binance chains; only ~$1.7M was swapped on DEX and considered at risk. IoTeX officially valued the loss at $4.4M (discounting frozen minted assets). The upgrade bypassed all cross-chain minting safeguards, allowing arbitrary token creation without collateral on the source chain. Funds were quickly moved and laundered.

exploits.description: Deployer private key leaked, attacker upgraded program and drained treasury
exploits.detailed_narrative: The attack combined oracle deception with social engineering (not pure key leak but pre-signed admin access). Over three weeks: (1) Attacker withdrew ETH from Tornado Cash (March 11) and deployed carbonvote token (CVT) on Solana (March 12, Pyongyang-time timestamp). (2) Seeded minimal liquidity on Raydium + wash trading to peg CVT at ~$1 (oracles treated it as valid collateral). (3) Used Solana durable nonces to socially engineer Security Council members into pre-signing 'routine' admin transactions (March 23–30). (4) On March 27 Drift migrated to 2-of-5 multisig and removed timelock (zero-delay execution). (5) April 1: attacker activated pre-signed txs, listed CVT as collateral, raised limits, deposited massive fake CVT, and withdrew real assets (JLP, USDC, SOL, wBTC, wETH) in 31 txs over 12 minutes. Funds swapped via Jupiter → bridged to Ethereum → ETH/USDC/Binance. Attributed to DPRK Lazarus Group. TVL dropped ~$1B across Solana DeFi; multiple downstream protocols paused or lost funds. No code bug—pure governance + oracle manipulation via human-targeted pre-signing.

exploits.description: Price manipulation of $THE token led to bad debt on Venus Protocol
exploits.detailed_narrative: (1) Nine-month preparation phase (June 2025 – March 2026): Attacker sourced 7,447 ETH (~$16.29M) through 77 Tornado Cash transactions, deposited as collateral on Aave, borrowing ~$9.92M in stablecoins, and quietly accumulated $THE representing 84% of Venus's 14.5M $THE supply cap. (2) Bypassing the supply cap (donation attack, March 15, 2026): Used six wallets to transfer ~36M $THE directly to the vTHE contract (bypassing mint()), inflating the vTHE exchange rate 3.81×, turning a ~$3.3M position into ~$12M nominal collateral. Normal deposits added 12.2M $THE (within cap), but donations pushed total to 53.2M $THE (367% of cap). Deployed attack contract (0x737bc98f1d34e19539c074b8ad1169d5d45da619) with borrowing permissions. (3) Price manipulation and recursive borrowing loop (March 15, 2026): With inflated collateral, borrowed CAKE, BNB, BTCB, USDC, etc., using them to buy more $THE on DEXes (violent price impact due to thin liquidity). $THE price pumped from ~$0.26 to ~$4 (spot peak), but Venus's Resilient Oracle (RedStone + Binance feeds) resisted via BoundValidator for ~37 minutes. Once feeds converged at ~$0.51 (double pre-attack price), loop escalated: Borrow → Buy $THE → Donate to vTHE → Inflate rate → Borrow more → Repeat. Peak: 53.2M $THE collateral against ~$14.9M borrowed (~$30M nominal value). (4) Extraction and implosion (March 15-16, 2026): ~50 transactions extracted assets over hours. Greed led to over-leveraging; $THE crashed to $0.22 (below starting point) due to sell pressure and liquidation. Position liquidated, but thin liquidity meant $30M nominal collateral yielded nothing recoverable. Bad debt: 1.18M CAKE and 1.84M THE stuck in protocol. (5) Post-attack developments: Venus paused markets, adjusted collateral factors for illiquid assets, committed to governance proposals for bad debt resolution, supply cap hardening, and re-audits. Attacker's funds parked; potential off-chain profits via CEX shorts untraceable.

exploits.description: Role-Play Attack exploiting AMM PnL accounting logic by playing both LP and trader roles atomically
exploits.detailed_narrative: The attacker exploited Denaria Finance's perp-DEX PnL settlement logic using a 'Role-Play Attack' pattern (arxiv.org/abs/2310.01081), simultaneously acting as liquidity provider and trader to game the protocol's internal accounting. Attack flow: (1) Flash-loaned 60K USDC from Aave on Linea. (2) Deployed two helper contracts, A and B. (3) Helper A deposited collateral and added liquidity via addLiquidity, becoming the dominant LP in the pool. (4) Helper B deposited collateral and opened a large position against A's liquidity, shifting the AMM's internal state—the settlement engine interpreted this movement as 'earned profit' for the LP. (5) Helper A settled PnL and called removeCollateral, withdrawing far more than its original deposit. The attack was executed in two rounds: Round 1 deposited 45K USDC and withdrew 183K USDC; Round 2 deposited 15K USDC and withdrew 42K USDC. After repaying the 60K USDC flash loan, net profit was 165,617 USDC. The entire attack was atomic, executed within a single transaction.

exploits.description: Missing hard slippage cap in Aave's new CoW Protocol swap integration allowed a $50M collateral swap to route through a $73K SushiSwap pool, destroying 99.93% of value
exploits.detailed_narrative: On March 12, 2026, a user attempted a routine collateral rotation on Aave—swapping ~$50.4M aEthUSDT into aEthAAVE—via mobile. The trade routed through Aave's new CoW Protocol integration (which had replaced the ParaSwap-based system in December 2025). (1) A CoW solver found a path: burn aEthUSDT → withdraw $50.4M USDT from Aave → swap to 17,957 WETH via Uniswap V3's deep USDT/WETH pool (clean execution) → push all WETH into the SushiSwap AAVE/WETH pool (0xD75EA151a61d06868E31F8988D28DFE5E9df57B4). (2) The SushiSwap pool held only 331.63 AAVE + 17.65 WETH (~$74K total liquidity). The trade pushed 17,957 WETH into a pool holding 17.65 WETH—1,017× the pool's entire reserve. (3) The AMM surrendered nearly all AAVE inventory: output was 327.24 AAVE (~$36K). The signed minimum buy amount was already 324.94 AAVE—the catastrophic route was baked in before settlement. Auto-slippage was 1.21%, meaningless on a 99% price-impact trade. (4) The interface showed warnings and required a confirmation checkbox; the user confirmed on mobile. (5) Value extraction cascade within the same block: an MEV bot backran (not sandwiched) the AAVE/WETH and USDT/WETH legs for ~$12.5M profit (harvest tx: 0x45388b0f9ff46ffe98a3124c22ab1db2b1764ecb3b61234e29e5c9732b7fd4ab), paying 13,087 ETH (~$30.2M) to Titan Builder; Titan's total block revenue was ~$34.3M; Lido (block proposer) received 568 ETH (~$1.2M); DEX LPs absorbed ~$3.5M passively. (6) Root cause: when Aave Labs replaced ParaSwap with CoW in December 2025, the old hard slippage cap (~30%) was not carried over. The teams who built the original protection (BGD Labs, ACI) were not consulted. DefiLlama's free LlamaSwap tool would have blocked the same trade entirely. (7) Aftermath: Aave returned $110,368 in collected fees and introduced 'Aave Shield' (25% price-impact hard block, on by default). CoW DAO refunded its fees and disclosed compounding failures: a stale hardcoded gas ceiling had rejected better solver quotes, a winning solver abandoned the order after two failed on-chain submissions, and the private RPC transaction showed evidence of mempool leakage (investigation ongoing). CoW also noted that even the best quotes implied ~90% loss on a $50M fill-or-kill order on an illiquid pair. The incident occurred days after a separate Aave misconfiguration (CAPO oracle) triggered $27.78M in wrongful wstETH liquidations on March 10. Community poll split blame: ~41% user, ~44% frontend, ~15% protocol.

exploits.description: Stale CAPO oracle parameters caused wstETH to be priced 2.85% below market, triggering $27.78M in wrongful liquidations of healthy E-Mode positions
exploits.detailed_narrative: On March 10, 2026, Aave's own Correlated Asset Price Oracle (CAPO) — a protective layer on top of Chainlink feeds designed to cap how fast the wstETH/stETH exchange rate could grow — misfired and liquidated 34 accounts holding 10,938 wstETH ($27.78M). No attacker was involved; the underlying Chainlink feed worked perfectly throughout. Root cause: CAPO relies on three parameters: snapshotRatio (anchor exchange rate), snapshotTimestamp (when it was set), and maxYearlyRatioGrowthPercent (growth cap). The wstETH CAPO contracts were initialized in early 2024 with a snapshotRatio of ~1.15. By March 2026, the real wstETH/ETH rate had grown to ~1.2285. (1) Chaos Labs' off-chain Edge Risk engine computed a correct snapshotRatio update to ~1.2282, with a timestamp set to 7 days prior (as designed). (2) However, an on-chain rate limiter capped any single update at 3% per 3 days. The existing on-chain ratio was ~1.1572, so the contract could only accept ~1.1919 — the oracle submitted this fallback value. (3) The timestamp, facing no such constraint, advanced to the 7-day-old reference point. This created a fatal mismatch: ratio anchored at ~1.1919 paired with a timestamp 7 days back. CAPO extrapolated forward from a number the ratio never reached, computing a ceiling of ~1.1939 — below the live market rate of ~1.2285. (4) Aave began pricing wstETH 2.85% below market. E-Mode positions (high LTV against correlated assets) were especially vulnerable — the small deviation pushed leveraged positions across liquidation thresholds. (5) AgentHub (BGD Labs' automated execution layer via Chainlink Automation) executed the parameter update one block after submission — no manual review, no human in loop. Parameter update tx: 0xfbafeaa8c58dd6d79f88cdf5604bd25760964bc8fc0e834fe381bb1d96d3db95; execution tx: 0x32c64151469cf2202cbc9581139c6de7b34dae2012eba9daf49311265dfe5a1e. (6) Liquidators captured ~512 ETH total: ~116 ETH from standard liquidation bonuses, ~13 ETH from fees, and ~382 ETH from pure arbitrage (buying wstETH at Aave's depressed price ~1.1939 and selling at market ~1.2285). (7) A near-identical mismatch had almost triggered ~1 month earlier but the CAPO wstETH agent wasn't connected yet — no alarm was raised. (8) Aftermath: Chaos Labs reduced wstETH borrow cap to 1 on Core and Prime instances (tx: 0x34f568b28dbcaf6a8272038ea441cbc864c8608fe044c590f9f03d0dac9cf7f8). 141.60 ETH recovered from Titan Builder + 13.32 ETH from liquidation fees. TokenLogic filed a Direct-to-AIP reimbursement proposal: 512.19 ETH total, 357.56 ETH net cost to DAO. The incident occurred amid major departures — BGD Labs (Feb 20) and ACI/Marc Zeller (March 3) both announced exits from Aave governance. Aave's Risk Oracle had streamed 1,200+ payloads across 3,000+ parameters without incident prior. No bad debt was incurred; full reimbursement planned.

exploits.description: First confirmed live exploit of deployed ZK cryptography — Groth16 verifier with γ=δ accepted any proof, enabling forged withdrawals
exploits.detailed_narrative: Veil Cash was a Tornado Cash fork on Base using Groth16 zk-SNARKs for private withdrawals. The deployed verifier had gamma (γ) and delta (δ) both set to the BN254 G2 generator — the snarkjs Phase 2 default placeholder that the toolchain expects users to replace during trusted setup. The CLI command that generates random values for γ and δ was never run. When γ=δ, the verifier's pairing equation collapses to checking 1=1, accepting any fabricated proof. (1) Attacker called withdraw() 29 times using fabricated nullifier hashes (0xdead0000 through 0xdead001c), draining 2.9 ETH from the 0.1 ETH pool in a single transaction. (2) Decurity's DefimonAlerts monitoring detected the attack and performed a whitehat rescue of the remaining 2.025 ETH from other pools (tx: 0x42019ae0532a25e40068fa16d2c7e3a9ce006f0b872d84ae5b4540b368a0268c). (3) The original exploiter returned the drained 2.9 ETH later the same day (tx: 0x5a16ada9fbb068cc2492df57694b25ea56734eb6599c8f269767a23d10e0d162). 100% of funds recovered. (4) DK27ss published a full PoC on GitHub. CoinsBench published detailed analysis. The exploit technique was now publicly documented. (5) Pashov Audit Group had audited Veil Cash, but the verifier contract was explicitly out of scope. This was the first confirmed live exploit of ZK cryptography in production, and served as the blueprint for the larger FoomCash drain days later. ZKSecurity and Dedaub subsequently scanned all EVM chains for contracts with the same δ=γ fingerprint; no other live contracts with significant value were found, but GitHub repositories carrying the vulnerable defaults still exist, including a Tornado Cash rebuild with 248 stars.

exploits.description: Identical Groth16 γ=δ misconfiguration as Veil Cash exploited at scale — $1.84M rescued by Decurity, $320K kept under protocol's own 'code is law' bounty, net loss $420K
exploits.detailed_narrative: FoomCash, a 'ZKProof-powered private lottery protocol' positioning itself as a Tornado Cash upgrade, had the identical Groth16 verifier misconfiguration as Veil Cash — gamma and delta both set to the BN254 G2 generator. The Phase 2 trusted setup CLI step was never run. Someone read the public Veil Cash post-mortem, identified FoomCash as a larger target with the same broken verifier, and scaled the attack. (1) On Base: whitehat 'duha' drained ~$320K in FOOM tokens in a single transaction sequence. FoomCash had published a bounty challenge on Bitcointalk with the rule 'THE ONLY RULE IS CODE. If your code can take the funds, YOU'VE WON.' duha claimed it was a legitimate bounty win. (2) On Ethereum: whitehat-rescue.eth (linked to security firm Decurity, address 0x46c403e3DcAF219D9D4De167cCc4e0dd8E81Eb72) independently identified the flaw and drained $1.84M in a claimed rescue operation. Decurity returned the funds the same day (tx: 0xfed2dc60634b321fab073312168b327a47fdd76d2a39489249e4ef986671e83f) and accepted a $100K security fee. (3) The exploit technique: set C = -vk_x (right side cancels to 1), use public constants A = α, B = β (left side also cancels to 1). The verifier accepts without needing a valid witness. On Base, 10 iterations drained 99.97% of FOOM; on Ethereum, 30 iterations drained 99.99%. (4) A public dispute erupted — duha entered the EthSecurity Telegram defending their right to keep funds, while FoomCash demanded 90% back. Resolution: duha kept ~$320K, Decurity returned $1.84M for $100K fee. Net loss: ~$420K. (5) FoomCash's social accounts had been dark since ~November 2025. Their March 1 post-mortem reframed the public standoff as an 'elite white-hat response.' Misconfigured verifiers: ETH (0xc043865fb4D542E2bc5ed5Ed9A2F0939965671A6), Base (0x02c30D32A92a3C338bc43b78933D293dED4f68C6). Total: 24.28 trillion FOOM tokens drained across both chains.

exploits.description: Missing ETH/USD multiplication in cbETH oracle priced a $2,200 asset at $1.12, triggering $1.78M in bad debt — code co-authored by Claude Opus 4.6
exploits.detailed_narrative: On February 15, 2026 at 18:01 UTC, governance proposal MIP-X43 executed on Moonwell, enabling Chainlink OEV wrapper contracts across markets on Base and Optimism. One configuration was wrong: the cbETH oracle pulled only the cbETH/ETH exchange rate (~1.12) and treated it as a dollar value, missing the ETH/USD multiplication that turns the ratio into a real price. cbETH, trading at ~$2,200, was suddenly reported at $1.12 — a 99.9% discount. (1) Within the same block, automated liquidation bots targeted every cbETH-backed position. 1,096.317 cbETH seized at the artificial price. A smaller group exploited the mispricing from the supply side — depositing minimal collateral and borrowing cbETH at $1.12. (2) Anthias Labs detected the discrepancy in 4 minutes and cut the borrow cap to 0.01, but liquidations continued because correcting the oracle required a 5-day governance voting and timelock period. (3) Total bad debt across 11 assets: cbETH ($1.03M), WETH ($479K), USDC ($233K), EURC ($11.6K), cbBTC ($11.4K), cbXRP ($7.9K), DAI ($1.5K), USDS ($1.1K), AERO ($204), MORPHO ($171), wstETH ($164). Total: $1,779,044.83. (4) The commit (PR #578) was co-authored by Claude Opus 4.6. GitHub Copilot reviewed all 4 changed files and generated 4 comments. The code passed human review, merged, and passed governance with 99.1% approval. Neither AI, nor humans, nor the DAO caught the missing multiplication. No price sanity check existed — no floor, no ceiling, no assertion that $1.12 for a $2,200 asset should halt deployment. (5) This was Moonwell's 3rd oracle failure in ~4 months: Oct 10 ($1.7M bad debt from mispriced volatile tokens), Nov 4 ($3.7M from wrsETH oracle reporting 1 wrsETH = 1,649,934 ETH after Balancer exploit), and now Feb 15 ($1.78M). ~$7M cumulative bad debt. Possibly the first major exploit of vibe-coded smart contracts. Mikko Ohtamaa tested feeding the same PR to Claude with a targeted prompt and concluded the error was catchable — but nobody asked the right question.

exploits.description: Nation-state threat landscape report: DPRK operatives infiltrating crypto companies as remote workers, China pre-positioning in critical infrastructure, and the shift from ransomware to digital parasitism
exploits.detailed_narrative: A threat intelligence report documenting the convergence of nation-state cyber operations targeting crypto and critical infrastructure. (1) DPRK 'Contagious Interview' campaign: operatives pose as recruiters from legitimate crypto companies on LinkedIn, walk candidates through fake interviews, and deliver BeaverTail/InvisibleFerret malware via 'technical assessments' (clone repo → npm install → backdoor). Also use hijacked Telegram accounts with looped video footage (not deepfakes) on fake Zoom/Teams calls to trick targets into downloading malware patches. Taylor Monahan (MetaMask): 'They've stolen over $300M via this method already.' Amazon blocked 1,800 fake IT worker applications traced to DPRK. Over 300 US companies unknowingly hired DPRK operatives. 76% of all crypto service compromises in 2025 traced to DPRK operations. $6.75B stolen by end of 2025 — not through code exploits but through people. (2) China's Volt Typhoon: embedded in US critical infrastructure (utilities, comms, transport) for 5+ years using living-off-the-land techniques. Salt Typhoon breached 9+ US telecoms including AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile; exploited CALEA wiretapping systems; intercepted calls from presidential campaign members. 80 countries affected, 600+ organizations notified. As of Feb 2026, attack may still not be fully remediated. (3) Picus Labs Red Report 2026: analyzed 1.1M malware samples; ransomware encryption dropped 38% as attackers shifted to stealth/persistence; credential theft from password stores in 23.49% of attacks; 80% of top techniques focus on stealth. (4) LummaC2 infostealer now uses trigonometry (measuring mouse movement angles) to detect sandboxes. Lazarus spent 17 days inside Safe{Wallet}'s infrastructure before the $1.5B Bybit extraction. The digital parasite model: don't detonate, don't announce — move in, feed on credentials, remain invisible.

exploits.description: Research exposing crypto press release infrastructure as primary legitimacy vector for scams — 62% of releases from high-risk/scam projects, enabling $17B in crypto scam losses in 2025
exploits.detailed_narrative: Chainstory analyzed 2,893 crypto press releases published June–November 2025 and found 62.5% came from projects flagged as high-risk or outright scams; only 27% from legitimate operations. The crypto press release ecosystem has become the primary tool for manufacturing legitimacy at scale. (1) The pipeline: project buys press release distribution (guaranteed placement on 100+ websites, no editorial review), influencers are paid $50–$60K per post to amplify (ZachXBT leaked a spreadsheet with 200+ influencer rate cards; fewer than 5 of 160+ disclosed posts as paid), bot services provide fake engagement to create illusion of organic interest. (2) Historical precedent: SEC pump-and-dump enforcement cases (2002–2015) showed press releases in 73.3% of schemes. Company insiders (CEOs/CFOs) in 60.7% of cases. Pattern persists: Christmas Eve 2025, fake 'CircleMetals' press release used Circle's branding and fabricated CEO quotes to run a wallet drainer. Hong Kong's JPEX exchange used press releases throughout 2023 to claim licensed status; 2,700 victims lost HK$1.6B. (3) The body count: CoinGecko tallied 53.2% of all crypto tokens as dead since 2021. 11.6 million tokens collapsed in 2025 alone — 86% of all recorded failures in crypto history. Q4 2025 buried 7.7M projects. (4) Enforcement declining: SEC crypto enforcement dropped 60% from 33 actions ($4.98B penalties) in 2024 to 13 actions ($142M) in 2025. (5) Crypto scams drained an estimated $17B in 2025. Operations buying bulk social media accounts are 238× more effective. AI-enabled scams extract 4.5× more money. Impersonation scams showed 1,400% YoY growth. The inverse signal: legitimate projects attract journalists; scam projects buy placements. More press releases from a single source = more questions to ask.

exploits.description: Forged cross-chain messages exploited a Hyperbridge validation flaw to seize admin control and mint 1B unbacked DOT on Ethereum
exploits.detailed_narrative: On April 13, 2026, an attacker exploited a validation flaw in Hyperbridge — a cross-chain bridge connecting Polkadot and Ethereum — to mint 1 billion DOT tokens on Ethereum and extract approximately $237K. Polkadot itself was not compromised; the vulnerability was confined to bridge infrastructure. (1) The attacker submitted a forged cross-chain message through Hyperbridge's verification system. Due to missing or bypassed validation checks, the system accepted the fabricated message as a legitimate cross-chain payload. (2) The forged message allowed the attacker to change administrator rights in the smart contract controlling DOT tokens on Ethereum, granting full control over token minting. (3) With admin privileges, the attacker minted 1 billion DOT on Ethereum — nominally worth over $1B but entirely unbacked by any locked assets on Polkadot. (4) The attacker attempted to dump the minted tokens on DEXes, but market liquidity was insufficient to absorb such volume. The massive sell pressure caused an immediate price collapse, and only ~$237K in ETH was successfully extracted. (5) The incident demonstrates the persistent gap between technical capability and economic reality in bridge exploits: creating billions in tokens is meaningless without market depth and buyer trust to convert them.

exploits.description: Integer overflow bug allowed fee parameter to wrap negative, protocol paid attacker to trade.
exploits.detailed_narrative:
Aftermath Finance lost $1.14M to an integer overflow bug. The fee was stored as u256 (always positive) but read elsewhere as signed (positive or negative). Passing a number close to the max, the signed reading wraps it to a huge negative, turning the fee into a rebate. The protocol pays you to trade.

Aftermath's perp source is private, but the exploit was reconstructed from the on-chain trace:
(1) Call create_integrator_info with u256 fee = 2^256 - 10^17 (looks like a huge positive number, reads as -10^17 signed).
(2) Place matching limit and market orders between two of their own accounts.
(3) Each trade fires PaidIntegratorFees — protocol sends USDC to the integrator (attacker).
(4) Atomic, repeatable, ~$79K profit per cycle.

Final on-chain proof from the exploit tx: PaidIntegratorFees event records: fees: 115792089237316195423570985008687907853269984665640563689698454007913129639936 (= -3.5×10^17 in signed i256), integrator_address: 0x1a65086c... (the attacker). The protocol logged itself paying a wrapped-negative fee to the exploiter. Function signature on-chain confirms: clearing_house::create_integrator_info(Address, U256) with no bounds check.

The April pattern of missing parameter bounds:
(1) Singularity: oracle fee tier set to 42 (Uniswap only supports 100/500/3000/10000)
(2) Aftermath: u256 fee parameter wraps to negative when read as signed
(3) Scallop: spool created without last_index initialization.
Same root cause across all three: setters that don't enforce the value range invariants the rest of the contract assumes. AI agents test boundary values automatically. Wrap a number, free money comes out.
